{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks about the effect of tipping a tooth on the periodontal ligament (PDL) fibers. Tipping refers to a movement of the tooth where it rotates or shifts in a direction, typically involving a combination of forces. In dental physiology, when a tooth tips, it undergoes a movement that involves both compression and tension on the PDL fibers. The fibers on the side of the tooth that is moving toward the bone (the side of the tip) are compressed, while the fibers on the opposite side (the side moving away from the bone) are stretched. This is a fundamental principle in tooth movement and periodontal mechanics. The PDL fibers are not uniformly compressed or stretched; instead, they experience differential forces. Therefore, the correct answer is 'half compressed half stretched'. This is consistent with biomechanical principles of tooth movement, where tipping results in a balanced distribution of forces across the PDL.", "answer_choice": "C"}
